video_title: The Strait of Hormuz Closure Is Messier Than You Think with Michael Every | TGS 223
gist: Rabobank strategist Michael Every argues the Strait of Hormuz will stay closed for months (into September) because the conflict pits a logical US 'art of the deal' against an immovable theological Iranian regime, and that markets are wrongly pricing a quick reopening. He frames the crisis as the final accelerant toward a fragmented, mercantilist, bloc-based world economy (a 'NAPA' Americas energy bloc vs an Asian bloc), a shift away from the dollar toward stablecoins, and a regime where supply availability—not price—becomes the binding constraint.

themes.theme: Hormuz reopening timeline and base case

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz will not reopen for months, with September flagged as when the crisis becomes acute, contrary to the market consensus pricing a near-term deal.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: If correct, energy markets and equities priced for a quick reopening face a large repricing; watch for the September window when drawn-down inventories stop masking the shortage.
insights.quote: I'm going to make that call that Horus is not going to be reopening for months and I'm sticking to that.

insights.claim: A US-led blockade/standoff is the most likely path because the US approaches the conflict logically (art of the deal) while Iran's regime is driven by theological refusal to concede, so the two positions cannot converge into a negotiated deal.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Investors expecting a Nash-equilibrium compromise are likely wrong; the binding variable is ideology, not market rationality, implying a longer risk-premium regime.
insights.quote: The other side is being theological and those two twains are never going to meet unfortunately.

insights.claim: Resolution likely requires escalation rather than diplomacy: either NATO physically clearing the strait with anti-drone/anti-missile ships and minesweepers, or China stepping in backstage with economic coercion once the crisis threatens its own interests.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Frames the catalysts to watch for de-escalation; absent these, the blockade persists, keeping a regional risk premium on oil.

insights.claim: A direct US military escalation against Iran, including tactical nuclear options, is judged extremely unlikely over the next few months because the US has passed multiple ideal windows and lacks appetite for direct confrontation.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Tail-risk of outright war is downweighted near-term, but the persistent blockade remains the base case driving prolonged disruption.

themes.theme: Game theory of the standoff and US strategic incentives

insights.claim: A US back-down ('TACO'—Trump always chickens out) would be the 'whole enchilada': as damaging to US geostrategic standing as the 1956 Suez crisis was to Britain and France, which is why the US cannot afford to capitulate.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Argues against the 'TACO' trade in this instance; a US retreat would signal a major power-handover to the Iran/Russia/China axis, repricing dollar and regional assets.
insights.quote: if the US does do a taco it's actually the whole enchilada in that it's as damaging to the US's geostrategic position as the Suez crisis was for the UK and France back in 1956

insights.claim: Having failed to achieve a quick regime-toppling 'Venezuela'-style win, the US's cynical optimal play is evolutionary 'spite'—keeping the strait constrained so the Iran/Russia/China bloc cannot capture Middle East energy, letting the US decide who gets supply.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Implies prolonged constrained supply is a strategically rational US outcome, not an accident—so betting on rapid normalization underweights US incentives to keep it closed.
insights.quote: it's actually better to have a long-running crisis where nobody gets very much and those who do, which is the US and allies are in a position to start deciding who does and who doesn't get energy.

insights.claim: Even a US back-down would not resolve the conflict; Israel and the UAE 'cannot go home' the way the US can because they live beside a regime sworn to destroy them, so they would act unilaterally and possibly extremely.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Means there is no clean off-ramp; even a diplomatic 'win' headline leaves embedded escalation risk in the region's energy infrastructure.

insights.claim: US midterm-election dynamics could incentivize Trump to engineer a temporary deal, but after the November vote the conflict could reignite, which Iran and Israel would both anticipate and pre-position for.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Any pre-midterm de-escalation should be treated as fragile and tactical, not a durable resolution—relevant for positioning oil and volatility around November.

themes.theme: Oil market mechanics and the wrong unit to watch

insights.claim: A regional Iranian risk premium will stay attached to Middle East oil until there is a decisive victor, because even westward pipeline alternatives through Saudi Arabia, Syria, Turkey or Israel/Jordan remain vulnerable to drone strikes and would take years to build.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Supports a structurally higher oil risk premium for an extended period regardless of headline resolution; pipeline-diversification bets won't quickly remove the premium.

insights.claim: Crude is the wrong single unit to watch; the binding constraints are specific distillates and byproducts—bunker fuel, diesel, jet fuel, naphtha for plastics, fertilizer, bitumen, sulfur, helium—each a critical input creating cascading, exponential supply failures.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Tracking only Brent/WTI misses the real risk; shortages in bunker fuel halt shipping and in helium halt chip and MRI production, so watch product-level availability not just crude price.
insights.quote: no helium, you're not making chips

insights.claim: China's sharp cut in oil imports is driven by running down its (opaquely large, state-secret) strategic inventories as import prices rose while it keeps refined-product consumer prices low, a buffer that must reverse by late summer when imports resume.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The current market calm is partly an inventory illusion; when Chinese restocking resumes (~late summer/September) demand returns just as supply is tightest, a potential price catalyst.

insights.claim: Oil may be leaking out of Hormuz quietly via a 'hush-hush' operation with transponders off, more than data shows, because official data is no longer reliable—meaning the true supply situation is genuinely unknowable.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Large error bands on actual throughput mean both bullish and bearish oil surprises are possible; treat reported flow figures with skepticism.

insights.claim: Iran still controls the Houthis and could close the Bab-el-Mandeb strait, which currently carries Red Sea oil at roughly half of normal Saudi diverted volumes; the Saudi cross-country pipeline alternative moves around 7.5 million barrels a day but has no outlet if that route closes.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A second chokepoint (Bab-el-Mandeb) is a live escalation lever; its closure would remove the current partial relief valve and re-tighten supply.

themes.theme: From price to availability: a Soviet-style supply economy

insights.claim: The constraint is shifting from price to availability—like a Soviet supermarket where bread is priced but absent—so screen prices for oil products become meaningless when no supply exists, forcing governments toward price controls.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A regime where availability, not price, clears markets breaks standard macro/CPI models and trading frameworks built on price signals; plan for quantity rationing risk.
insights.quote: what's the point in having a price if you don't have any bloody product?

insights.claim: If shortages bite, the likely policy response is geopolitical rationing—organizing blocs of energy-producing, refining and consuming countries that balance internally and don't export outside the bloc, creating, e.g., $50 oil inside one bloc and $250 oil outside.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Implies bifurcated regional energy pricing rather than a single global price—an enormous structural change for global macro, FX and commodity arbitrage.

insights.claim: Macroeconomists track demand-side abstractions (consumer demand, government spending, gross fixed capital formation) but have no dataset on physical supply—what is made, with what inputs, sourced from where—and militaries will soon force the creation of such supply-security data.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Signals a coming institutional pivot to supply-chain/physical-input intelligence; firms and investors with that visibility gain an edge as policy reorients to supply security.

themes.theme: Mercantilism, bloc fragmentation and a NAPA energy hub

insights.claim: The Hormuz crisis is the final accelerant pushing the world toward mercantilism and a splintering of the global economy into two smaller blocs (broadly East and West) with separate supply chains, partners and metabolisms.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A multi-year de-globalization / bloc-formation theme reshapes trade, supply chains and asset correlations; position for fragmentation rather than re-integration.
insights.quote: I had no idea that the Horus crisis would be the final accelerant that gets us there.

insights.claim: The Americas could coalesce into a 'NAPA' (North American Petroleum hub) energy bloc—Canada, Mexico, US plus Venezuela, Colombia, Argentina and Brazil (election-dependent)—making the bloc a net energy exporter with cheap domestic energy and leverage over the rest of the world.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Brazil's October election is a pivot point for the bloc's energy balance; a US-favorable result would strengthen the Americas energy bloc—watch as a geopolitical catalyst.

insights.claim: Asia is structurally short of both food and energy; peeling off Japan, South Korea and the Philippines into the US-aligned bloc leaves India and China scrambling, with India tilting toward a UAE/Israel energy-and-defense axis and China the central unresolved supplier question.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: India's bloc alignment and China's energy sourcing are the key swing variables determining Asian bloc cohesion and Middle East demand competition.

insights.claim: Fragmentation sacrifices the comparative-advantage efficiency of globalization (smaller aggregate output) but could narrow domestic inequality—genuine Ricardian trade within balanced blocs ends the rich-richer/poor-poorer dynamic and could deliver boom times for working and middle classes.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Reframes de-globalization as potentially positive for labor over capital and for productive investment over financialization—relevant to long-run sector and asset allocation.
insights.quote: instead of making money from money, you're going to have to invest your money in making things from upstream to downstream

insights.claim: No deal with China on Hormuz/energy is likely until the Russia-Ukraine war and China's East Asia demands (Taiwan, regional security) are settled simultaneously, like a Monopoly game where all players must complete their sets before trading.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Links Hormuz resolution to unrelated theaters; a durable Middle East settlement may be hostage to Russia-Ukraine and Taiwan, extending the timeline and uncertainty.

insights.claim: The dollar's reserve role is structurally doomed by the Triffin dilemma: the dollar is backed by the US military, the military by US industry, but global demand for US financial assets (not goods) forces trade deficits and de-industrialization, so the dollar ultimately destroys the rules-based order it underpins.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A core bear case for the dollar's reserve status and the rules-based order; frames de-dollarization as structural rather than cyclical.
insights.quote: everybody wants US financial assets, not US goods. Erggo, you have to run a trade deficit. Erggo, you have to de-industrialize

insights.claim: Stablecoins are the inevitable plan B: the US will use legacy financial power to transition toward a stablecoin-based dollar, forcing energy/food/defense buyers in the US-led bloc to pay in stablecoins—digital tokens backed by US debt that lower US interest rates and are a 'buy one, get one free' for the US.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Bullish structural thesis for US-backed stablecoins as a geopolitical settlement layer and a tool to suppress US rates—watch stablecoin policy as a dollar-transition mechanism.
insights.quote: we want to be paid in stable coins rather than euro dollars, that changes the entire global dynamic

insights.claim: The US can leverage legacy financial power ('fartcraft') to acquire physical assets and chokepoints—e.g., arbitrarily valuing land, borrowing 10-to-1 against it on Wall Street, and using the proceeds to buy resource-rich, geostrategic territory like Greenland.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Illustrates how financial engineering converts into hard-asset and supply-chain control—a lens for anticipating US resource and territorial acquisition moves.

insights.claim: The crisis is already producing higher inflation upstream-to-downstream in plastics, packaging and other unsexy producer inputs, even where consumer life still feels normal, signaling the underlying crisis is not yet being dealt with.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Producer-level input inflation is a leading indicator before it hits shelves; watch packaging/plastics/fertilizer costs as early warning of broader consumer inflation.

insights.claim: Shortages will hit Asia before Europe, poorer countries before richer, and by fuel type—refined products and bunker fuel are the chokepoints, since no bunker fuel means no ship movement and a cascading collapse of international cargo.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Provides a sequencing map for where economic stress and political instability appear first—monitor poorer Asian economies and bunker-fuel availability as leading stress signals.

insights.claim: Markets are complacent because the US and Japan are drawing down inventories that paper over the crisis—'when they're gone, they're gone'—analogous to a high earner who lost their job but keeps spending on credit until reality forces rapid adjustment.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The 'stocks at all-time highs' calm is an inventory-financed illusion; the risk is a sudden, sharp repricing once buffers exhaust, plausibly around September.

insights.claim: If price stops working as a rationing mechanism, governments will resort to quantity rationing, and ordinary individuals should pre-emptively build alternative local/seasonal food and lower-energy lifestyle buffers to avoid supermarket-shock scenarios.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Tail-risk preparedness signal—supply availability shocks at the consumer level are a non-zero scenario, favoring local/resilient supply chains.
insights.quote: Ration first and beat the rush.

themes.theme: Iran endgame, ideology and analytical method

insights.claim: The litmus test for any deal is whether Iran hands over its uranium; if it refuses, the US fallback is satellite surveillance with a standing threat to bomb any approach to the stockpile, which precludes a negotiated reopening of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Uranium handover is the single observable that would shift the base case; its absence keeps the blockade/escalation paths dominant.
insights.quote: If they don't hand over the uranium, the only fallback option that the US has... is to say, "We know where it is. We're watching it by satellite. As soon as anyone goes towards it, we'll bomb it."

insights.claim: Iran's regime does not care about its own people—evidenced by a January massacre of 30,000-35,000 citizens and a self-inflicted water/drought crisis from diverting funds to Hezbollah, Hamas and the Houthis—so humanitarian pressure is unlikely to force concessions.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Removes humanitarian collapse as a near-term resolution channel; regime change via internal uprising is possible but could also fracture Iran and further destabilize the region's oil.

insights.claim: The biggest analytical error markets make is refusing to price ideology/theology—assuming a Nash equilibrium or negotiated compromise always emerges—when in this conflict neither side will back down.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Frameworks assuming rational compromise systematically misprice this conflict; the durable edge is incorporating ideological intransigence into base cases.
insights.quote: everyone talks about Nash equilibriums... No, that's not always the way.

insights.claim: China will not pivot to consumption for ideological reasons despite the logical case, just as US 'socialist' policies once deemed impossible (QE, state ownership of AI shares) are now mainstream as the Overton window collapses and left and right converge.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Repeated 'China consumption pivot' calls are perennially wrong; meanwhile US state intervention in strategic sectors (e.g., AI equity stakes) is becoming policy-relevant.
insights.quote: We've had Bernie Sanders last week saying 50% of AI shares should be stateowned. And we've had Trump saying the same thing

insights.claim: In great-power contests hard power outweighs soft power: marginally better soft power only decides between two roughly equal hard-power blocs, so US reputational damage from the crisis matters less than its material/military leverage.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Downweights 'declining US credibility' narratives as market drivers relative to physical/military control of supply—useful for weighting geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: soft power doesn't matter as much as hard power

insights.claim: Energy is the floor under the economy and technology the ceiling, yet economists have functioned as the 'shaman' of civilization while taking energy for granted—a disconnect much of Wall Street still does not grasp.
insights.speaker: Nate Hagens
insights.so_what: Reinforces an energy-as-primary-constraint lens for valuing the whole economy, implying energy availability shocks have outsized, underpriced systemic effects.
insights.quote: energy is the floor and we've taken it for granted and I think a lot of people on Wall Street still don't understand that.
